content.problemStatement:
Alice has an array AAA of length NNN, and her goal is to rearrange the array in a way that minimises the maximum subarray sum of the resulting array.

However, Alice must keep the relative order of all non-negative and negative elements the same as in the original array. In other words:


The non-negative numbers should appear in the same order as they originally appeared in AAA.
The negative numbers should appear in the same order as they originally appeared in AAA.
You are tasked with finding the minimum possible value of the maximum subarray sum after rearranging the array according to these rules.

**Note:** The sum of an empty subarray is 000.


Input Format

The first line of input will contain a single integer TTT, denoting the number of test cases.
Each test case consists of multiple lines of input.

The first line of each test case contains a integer NNN — the length of the array.
The second line of each test case contains NNN space-separated integers - values of the array AAA .




Output Format
For each test case, output on a new line the minimum possible value of the maximum subarray sum after rearranging the array according to these rules.
constraints: The sum of NNN over all test cases won't exceed 2⋅1052\cdot 10^52⋅105.

sampleTestCases.explanation:
Explanation:
Test case 111: There can be many other ways; one such rearrangement is:

[1,2,−1,−2]→[1,−1,−2,2][1, 2, -1, -2] \xrightarrow{} [1, -1, -2, 2][1,2,−1,−2]​[1,−1,−2,2] and final maximum subarray sum will be 222. It can be proven that this is the least possible following the rules.

Test case 222: Since all the elements are negative, we can choose an empty subarray, which results in a maximum subarray sum of 000.
Test case 333: There can be many other ways; one such rearrangement is:

[−2,3,0,2]→[3,0,−2,2][-2, 3, 0, 2] \xrightarrow{} [3, 0, -2, 2][−2,3,0,2]​[3,0,−2,2] and final maximum subarray sum will be 333. It can be proven that this is the least possible following the rules.
